data.title: Cracker Barrel's recovery may be 'protracted,' as younger diners stay away, analysts say
data.description:
By Bill Peters

'We see risk that efforts to evolve the brand have been stymied, at least temporarily,' BofA says, as restaurant chain deals with rebranding fallout

Shares of Cracker Barrel are down 13.4% so far this year.

After facing anger from customers, Cracker Barrel Old Country Store Inc. has walked back a new logo and new restaurant designs in an effort to keep its customers. But those efforts have so far come up short, and some analysts say the Americana-heavy restaurant chain's recovery could happen later rather than sooner.

That assessment arrived after Cracker Barrel (CBRL) on Wednesday forecast fewer diners over the fiscal year ahead, even as it tries to take steps to encourage more customer feedback and lean into nostalgia-driven marketing.

Chief Financial Officer Craig Pommells, during Cracker Barrel's earnings call on Wednesday, said that customer traffic has fallen around 8% since Aug. 19, when the company initially changed its old logo to remove a man sitting next to a barrel to the left of Cracker Barrel's name. BofA analyst Sara Senatore, in a note on Thursday, said the traffic decline "suggests that recovery may be protracted."

Cracker Barrel restored the old logo days after the change, following outrage from diners, conservatives who claimed the change was "woke," and even President Donald Trump, who said the company should go back to its old signage. This month, the company also halted remodels, after resistance from customers.

Pommells, during Wednesday's earnings call, said that while the consumer retreat from its restaurants had been broad-based, they had seen "a little bit less decline" among diners over the age of 65. But to Senatore, that was still bad news.

"With younger consumers reacting more strongly than older consumers (65+), we see risk that efforts to evolve the brand have been stymied, at least temporarily," she said.

Elsewhere, Truist analyst Jake Bartlett said in a note on Wednesday that the fallout from the rebranding efforts has been "severe with no sign of let-up." But he said there were ways the chain could still make a comeback.

He said more marketing around its fall menu and the college football season, as well as what the company described as "big news coming next week" might help build attention. Executives, during the call on Wednesday, also said they had brought back once-popular menu items, while introducing newer ones and taking steps to make sure its biscuits taste better.

Cracker Barrel faces competition from other sit-down chains that have gotten more aggressive on discounting, and has been trying to improve food quality and service. But Bartlett said he still liked the stock.

"We would be buyers on post-earnings weakness," Bartlett said, "given our view that the core drivers of CBRL's brand turnaround are intact (improved menu innovation and service) and that sales may start to recover from the rebranding backlash soon with the resumption of the fall menu marketing."

Shares of Cracker Barrel finished 7.6% lower on Thursday. The stock is down 13.4% so far this year.

-Bill Peters

data.title: S&P 500 Could Hit 7,200 By 2026, Wells Fargo Says In Aggressive Upgrade
data.description:
Sep 17 - Wells Fargo pushed its S&P 500 price targets higher this week, lifting the year-end 2025 target to 6,650 from 6,3006,500 and moving its 2026 forecast to 7,200 from 6,9007,100.

The bank ties the upgrade partly to continued AI-driven capital spending and expects a shallower path for rates if the Federal Reserve starts cutting, markets price a possible 25-basis-point move next week.

Other firms have nudged estimates higher too: Barclays raised its outlook to 6,450, while Deutsche Bank set a loftier 7,000 target. Those calls reflect a wider Wall Street view that AI investment can support earnings even as investors debate valuation risks.

Wells Fargo's chief equity strategist, Ohsung Kwon, warned of froth in some pockets but added that if AI capex holds, the broader rally can keep going. The bank also cites potential policy tailwinds such as deregulation and proposed tax changes.

data.title: Olin Ends Mitsui Joint Venture in Pivot Toward Vinyls
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Olin and Mitsui have agreed to end their Blue Water Alliance joint venture by the end of this year.

The two companies will continue to work together on strategic opportunities under a more flexible framework that supports Olin's evolving participation in Ethylene Dichloride, Olin said Thursday.

The decision is aimed to help Olin broaden its chlorine derivatives optionality and reduce its spot exposure to the merchant Ethylene Dichloride market, Olin Chlor Alkali Products & Vinyls President Deon Carter said. Instead, Olin is planning to grow its participation in the vinyls market.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: Nvidia Changes the Equation for Intel. For the Stock, the Big Problem Remains. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Adam Levine

Both rivals and partners, Nvidia and Intel made a deal this week that included an Intel share sale to Nvidia for $5 billion and partnerships around PC and data center chips. Investors are reading the tea leaves and see more: that Nvidia will come in and save Intel's struggling manufacturing business.

The deal solves four problems for Intel, but it doesn't address the root of Intel's woes: its manufacturing technology, which has fallen behind Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing.

The first thing Intel gets is more cash that it needs for capital expenditures. The company has put significant delays on planned construction, curtailing capex in 2025 by 25% in the first half of the year. The capex is a necessary ingredient for Intel's plans to catch up to Taiwan Semi, though the main issues are still technical.

So far this year, Intel has received $5.7 billion from the U.S, Department of Commerce for shares priced at $20.74. That gave investors confidence that the U.S. government wouldn't let Intel fail, putting its finger on the scale. Soon after, SoftBank added $2 billion at $23 per share, and now Nvidia is paying $5 billion at $23.28.

All told, Intel is issuing 576 million new shares for $12.7 billion, diluting shareholders by 12%. But they brought something more important than cash: the implicit backing of powerful players.

And that brings the second thing Intel gets from Nvidia: investor confidence. While these deals have been made at a discount to Intel's then market price, the shares have soared anyway. This is about investors deciding that Intel isn't going anywhere. The U.S. government, Nvidia, and SoftBank are now keenly interested in Intel's survival. For investors, that's made betting on Intel's cheap stock seem like a good idea.

The third thing Intel gets from Nvidia is a better PC chip, which will now have an Intel CPU and an Nvidia GPU, with Nvidia's high-speed connection pulling it all together. Those chips will also offer better gaming and on-device AI, key marketing points in the PC world.

And fourth, the partnership helps Intel in the data center, where the company's CPUs long dominated inside servers. But AI demand has changed the equation, with hundreds of billions of dollars in new investment going primarily to Nvidia's GPUs used for AI computing.

At first, the common setup for an AI server was two Intel CPUs linked to eight Nvidia GPUs. But then Nvidia began making its own CPU based around technology from Arm Holdings, not the x86 platform used by Intel and Advanced Micro Devices. That move effectively cut out Intel entirely from the AI data center.

In a March interview, Arm's senior vice president of infrastructure, Mohamed Awad, predicted that Arm data center CPUs would raise their market share to 50% by the end of the year, up from 15% in 2024. Those gains have largely come at Intel's expense.

Using a joint design with Nvidia, Intel's CPUs could be back in the AI server mix, and Intel could recover some of its lost business.

All of this is good for Intel, but probably not $28 billion good, the market value generated on Thursday from the stock's 23% price jump.

Jordan Klein, a tech and media sector specialist at Mizuho, thinks investors should avoid the stock for now. "I can see momentum chasing INTC for [the] short term but to me this is not some rerating of stock or major guide up for growth for them," he wrote to clients today. "Personally, I am not yet ready to sell other high quality semis to start chasing INTC up 25%+ today." Among those higher quality names, Klein cited Nvidia, Taiwan Semiconductor, Broadcom and memory chip makers.

Intel's biggest problem remains its foundry business, a moonshot program that began four years ago, in which Intel hoped to make advanced chips for designers across the world. The idea was to give Apple, Samsung, Nvidia, and others an alternative to Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturer. So far, it has been a bust.

Intel still lacks a big outside customer for its foundry, and its manufacturing remains behind Taiwan Semi. In the company's recent quarterly earnings call, Intel's new CEO, Lip-Bu Tan, said that the costs associated with the next generation of technology couldn't be supported by Intel's sales alone.

Intel needs outside customers to keep the business moving forward. That's one thing Nvidia hasn't promised. It could still happen, but it's far from guaranteed.

Tan and Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang were asked several times about that possibility during a press conference on Thursday. The CEOs shied away from the issue in several responses. But Huang did use the opportunity to heap praise on Taiwan Semi's manufacturing, calling it "magic."

Intel's executives — and shareholders — should be feeling better about the company this week, but no one is calling the company magical. At least not yet.

Write to Adam Levine at adam.levine@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Beyond Meat Wins Arbitration Proceeding Against Manufacturer Seeking $73M
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Beyond Meat won its arbitration proceeding against a former co-manufacturer that was trying to win $73 million in damages.

The plant-based meat company also received an interim award from the arbitrator, who found the company had a valid basis to terminate its agreement with the co-manufacturer, Beyond Meat said Thursday in a filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission. The amount of the award was not disclosed.

The co-manufacturer had been responsible for producing products on behalf of Beyond Meat under an agreement that was terminated in November 2023. That deal was ended because the manufacturer failed to produce food in compliance with applicable laws and as required by the agreement, Beyond Meat said.

The co-manufacturer brought action against Beyond Meat in a confidential arbitration proceeding in March 2024. It alleged that Beyond Meat had terminated the agreement without basis and therefore owed the manufacturer payments. It claimed $73 million in total damages.

Beyond Meat filed counter claims in October 2024, alleging breach of contract, breach of the duty of good faith and fear dealing, and along with claims.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1422 ET - Workday's core business continues to pose a large growth opportunity, D.A. Davidson analysts Gil Luria and Lucky Schreiner say in a note. The company's management sees a large addressable market of $188 billion for its core products, compared to $160 billion last year, given under penetrated markets with U.S. federal customers and frontline workers, the analysts say. The company is also working to simplify its platform to gain traction with mid-sized enterprises, which represent over half of the total available market, they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1418 ET - Workday's new product announcements and financial disclosures put it in a better position to reap the benefits of growing agentic AI adoption across human resources, D.A. Davidson analysts Gil Luria and Lucky Schreiner say in a note. Workday is making it easier for customers to adopt new AI agents, and has been able to buy AI tools that can work off its curated, high quality enterprise data, the analysts say. It also requires agents to have a measured return on investment before release, they say. However, with some of its new AI agents set for release next year, further revenue growth will come mostly from acquisitions in the meantime, limiting upside to near-term numbers, they say. They raise their price target on stock to $260 from $225.Workday is down 0.6% to $233.57. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1408 ET - Sangoma Technologies' return to growth is taking longer than expected, TD Cowen's David Kwan says in a report as the stock sinks 14% to C$7.27 after FY26 guidance came in soft. Sangoma's revenue outlook of C$200 million-C$210 million was 4% below expectations at midpoint, the analyst says. However, the stock may already have poor performance baked in. "We believe this is mostly reflected in the current discount valuation," the analyst says. Shares are down 27% year-to-date, which Kwan says makes the valuation "attractive, along with its [roughly] 15% free cash flow yield," as "execution and visibility are improving." (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1308 ET - CrowdStrike's new solutions and features show it continues to innovate at an impressive pace, Junaid Siddiqui of Truist Securities says in a note. The company introduced seven new AI agents to automate security workflows at its recent conference, as well as a no-code platform for customers to build their own security agents, among other innovations, the analyst says. Those new solutions and features should help the company to benefit from a number of continued tailwinds, including vendor consolidation and a heightened threat environment, Siddiqui says, raising the price target to $550 from $500. CrowdStrike climbs 11% at $496.34.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1250 ET - CrowdStrike's flexible subscription licensing model for its Falcon platform appears to be boosting the company's traction with both enterprise and small- and mid-sized businesses, JPMorgan analysts say in a note. Flex remained a central theme throughout the company's recent conference, both in conversations with customers and partners as well as during presentations, the analysts say. "Investor sessions and customer panels reinforced CrowdStrike's position as the 'oxygen' of cybersecurity, with Flex contracts driving deeper module adoption, larger deal sizes, and streamlined procurement," they say. CrowdStrike surges 11%.(kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1200 ET — CrowdStrike remains the "gold standard" in cybersecurity, with its new AI products positioning it to lead the cybersecurity industry in agentic AI, Wedbush Securities analysts say in a note. That's especially important as organizations face increasingly complex cybersecurity threats, with a majority of organizations expecting a 20% increase in non-human interactions next year, the analysts say. "With the threat landscape now allowing conventionally underskilled adversaries to leverage AI and increase sophistication of malware, CRWD provides security teams necessary tools to combat these attacks by positioning its customers for the modern battlefield," they say. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1110 ET - FCC Chair Brendan Carr tells CNBC that he thinks Jimmy Kimmel's comments about the political affiliation of Charlie Kirk's alleged assassin appeared to "directly mislead the American public." Carr's claim comes a day after Disney's ABC suspended "Jimmy Kimmel Live!" in response to Kimmel speculating that Tyler Robinson, the alleged killer of the far-right ideologist, was connected to Donald Trump's MAGA movement. Carr says on CNBC that the FCC's broadcast TV licenses come with "an obligation to operate in the public interest." Carr praised Nexstar Media Group and Sinclair, the owners of some ABC TV stations, for saying they would drop Kimmel's show over the comments. Nexstar is seeking approval for its acquisition of fellow broadcaster Tegna. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1033 ET - Meta showcased its new Meta Ray-Ban Display smart glasses yesterday and while the product is clearly improving, it's still a ways away from becoming mainstream and won't challenge Apple's wearable devices, says Oppenheimer analyst Martin Yang. The $800 price of the glasses is a step toward making them accessible, but their weight at 67 grams is still burdensome, Yang says. The technology required to reduce the weight is still far from meeting commercial pricing and volume. As a result, Meta's glasses won't yet pose a threat to Apple's ecosystem around iPhone, AirPods and Apple Watch, Yang says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0951 ET - National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett said on CNBC that he wasn't aware of anyone in the government negotiating with Nvidia to encourage it to invest in Intel. The U.S. announced it was taking a 10% stake in Intel last month and Hassett says Intel wasn't meeting the marks it needed to in order to receive funds from the CHIPS Act. "Instead of just throwing money away into a business that may or may not succeed, the U.S. government is helping the business succeed and making it so that the taxpayer benefits if they're successful at that," Hassett says. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0926 ET - Dutch supplier of chip-making equipment ASML Holding could benefit from a partnership between Nvidia and Intel to jointly develop data-center and personal-computing products, ING's Jan Frederik Slijkerman says. Intel is a longtime customer of ASML's lithography tools, which are essential to produce semiconductors. ASML shares jumped nearly 8% after Nvidia said it would inject $5 billion into Intel as part of their collaboration. Slijkerman says the increase in ASML's stock price shows investors expect more demand for lithography tools. ASML shares trade 7.3% higher at 790.80 euros. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

0919 ET - Getting a $5 billion investment from Nvidia is a game-changer for Intel that now positions it firmly in the AI market, say Wedbush analysts. "Along with the recent US Government investment for 10% this has been a golden few weeks for Intel after years of pain and frustration for investors," the analysts say. A partnership with Nvidia, which has the most advanced chips in the world, positions Intel to meaningfully contribute to AI development in the U.S. "Today's announcement further strengthens the US lead in the AI Arms Race against China as Intel now goes from a laggard to a catalyst," the analysts say. Intel surges 29% premarket. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

0809 ET - Intel is getting another high-profile boost. A month after Softbank said it was investing $2 billion and the U.S. government said it was taking a 10% stake in the troubled chipmaker, Intel is now getting a $5 billion investment from Nvidia. The company has fallen behind competitors, who have emerged to lead the lucrative business of supplying AI chips. Unlike the U.S. government's stake, Nvidia's investment could provide a boost in actual chip development capability that Intel investors have been looking for by giving the company a powerful partner in the chip industry. Intel surges 29% premarket. (nicholas.miller@wsj.com)

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0921 ET - Novo Nordisk has clear leadership in the field of orally-taken obesity treatments following trial data for its Wegovy weight-loss pill and a competing tablet from Eli Lilly, UBS analyst Matthew Weston writes. Lilly had already released headline data for its Orforglipron pill, delivering up to 12.4% weight-loss at the highest dose, below the 16.6% weight-loss in a trial of Novo Nordisk's pill, Weston adds. Further data at this week's EASD medical conference in Austria showed Orforglipron weight-loss plateaued relatively early and even saw weight regain at the 12 milligram dose, he adds. "Orforglipron still presents a threat on being a more scalable product, and therefore potentially discounted price." It also doesn't require 30 minutes fasting when taking it, so could be seen as more convenient, he says. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)Abhishek Raval is an analyst at AlphaValue. "Novo Nordisk's Prospects Could Turn Around — Market Talk," at 1200 GMT, misstated the firm's name.

0800 ET - Novo Nordisk's efficacy edge in its new obesity pills on top of Wegovy's additional health benefits should stem the Danish pharmaceutical company's market-share erosion and turn around its prospects, AlphaValue's Abhishek Raval writes. New weight-loss candidate CagriSema's healthy commercial potential and swiftly acting new management should also help, he adds. The massive collapse in Novo Nordisk's share price over the past 15 months, due to competitive concerns, pricing pressure and a few other factors, seems largely overdone to AlphaValue. "The current valuation levels bake in excessive fears, providing a good opportunity for contrarian investors." Shares rise 7.2%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com) Corrections & Amplifications

This article was corrected at 08:32 a.m. ET to clarify that Abhishek Raval is an analyst at AlphaValue. An earlier article misstated the firm's name.

0710 ET - Sales of Meta Platforms' smartglasses are going to continue to accelerate, and this will also benefit fourth-quarter revenue for its partner, Ray-Ban maker EssilorLuxottica, RBC Capital Markets analyst Piral Dadhania says. Meta released a new pair of smartglasses with artificial intelligence on Wednesday--a newer version of its Ray-Ban smartglasses and athlete-specific smartglasses with Oakley, which is also owned by EssilorLuxottica. The decision to build out a smartglasses product range across different prices is positive, Dadhania says. The smartglasses sell for between $379 to $799. Shares in Meta are up 0.7% in U.S. premarket trading, and Paris-listed shares in EssilorLuxottica are up 1.6%. (aimee.look@wsj.com)

0337 ET - Roche's acquisition of 89bio adds a potential blockbuster to its drug pipeline, Vontobel's Stefan Schneider writes. The Swiss healthcare company said it will acquire the U.S. biopharmaceutical group for up to $3.5 billion. 89bio is developing a potentially best-in-class fatty liver treatment, aligning the deal with its plans to expand its weightless treatment offerings. Revenues for the drug, pegozafermin, could hit $2 billion in 2030, giving Roche a potential blockbuster that also avails itself to combination therapies with existing Roche drugs, Vontobel says. Roche shares are steady at 260.30 Swiss francs. (william.gray@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0756 ET - The earnings drivers behind Allianz's growth are stronger than initially expected and are set to continue delivering well beyond the end of its current plan in 2027, Berenberg says. The German insurance giant's three key drivers are its target to reduce its non-life cost ratio by 30 basis points per year, the increase in operating capital generation on more efficient capital allocation at country level and self-funding growth in its life-insurance division. The third-quarter outlook is strong, analyst Michael Huttner writes. "We believe that net inflows at PIMCO remain strong, that pricing in motor in France and Germany is continuing to improve...and that there are continuing high margins in Italian non-life," he notes, adding this should more than offset the effect of a weaker U.S. dollar. Shares are up 0.35% at 345.7 euros. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0742 ET - Merger and acquisition activity in the European banking sector is set to continue, according to a new report by law firm White & Case. This will be driven by what the report describes as an ideal alignment with banks returning to non-government hands, the reduction of bad-loan portfolios, better capitalizations and a period of high interest rates having filled up their M&A war chests. Challenger bank consolidation will also continue given the sharper focus on profitability and appetite among private-equity owners for exits, it notes. Germany and Poland are expected to join Italy and Spain in the spotlight. "The floodgates of European bank public M&A are open...H2 2025 and H1 2026 will be anything but dull," partner Hyder Jumabhoy says. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0259 ET - CVC Capital Partners' potential change of leadership shouldn't affect the stock, Citi says in a research note after reports that the global buyout company is stepping up CEO-succession planning ahead of its fundraising cycle. The cycle is expected to start in 2027. "A key attraction of CVC shares for investors has been the strength and depth of CVC's network and senior team and well-embedded strategy, which should imply strategy continuity, regardless of any potential change," analyst Nicholas Herman writes. Leadership changes can sometimes create share-price volatility, especially as investors have a favorable view of CEO Rob Lucas, but in this case no material share reaction is expected, he says. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0114 ET - Iluka Resources, now in "cash harvest mode," could consider offloading its stake in Deterra Royalties, according to Macquarie analysts. "While we understand divestment of DRR stake could result in capital gains tax, the mineral sands market headwind present another opportunity for ILU to review its portfolio and recycle capital," the analysts say in a note. Iluka has a 20% stake in Deterra, a business it spun off in 2020. In an August investor presentation, Iluka said Deterra provides it with an additional source of long-term financial strength. It estimated the value of its stake at A$445 million. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2356 ET - A U.S. rate cut could support emerging-market assets, according to J.P. Morgan Asset Management's Kerry Craig, adding that the Fed's 25bp cut was widely expected. Lower rates should mean a weaker U.S. dollar, which is likely to support equity and local currency debt among assets in emerging markets, the strategist notes. The lower risk around a U.S. recession also means that credit markets should remain well supported, he adds. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

2347 ET - The Indonesian government's 200 trillion rupiah liquidity injection into state-owned banks appears to be a positive development over the mid-to-long term for the banking sector, CGS International economists write in a note. The Ministry of Finance's move is intended to push credit growth, among various factors, they note. With the injection of liquidity, funding competition for special deposit rates among banks is expected to come down. "The biggest beneficiaries will be banks that can deploy loans more quickly without compromising loan yields or asset quality, in our view," they say.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1521 ET - Oil futures add to yesterday's losses as the high-demand summer season begins to wind down and the market waits to see what if any additional sanctions are placed on Russian oil. The big decline in U.S. crude stocks reported by the EIA is attributed mostly to the jump in exports. "While the drawdown in crude inventories was impressive, it seems to be mostly export driven with the help of near multi-year lows in the U.S. dollar, and not necessarily from domestic demand," Dennis Kissler of BOK Financial says in a note. WTI settles down 0.7% at $63.57 a barrel, which Kissler sees as the near-term support. Brent falls 0.8% to $67.44 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1330 ET - Crude futures are losing ground for a second straight session as the market turns attention back to supply and demand. "People are talking about too much supply chasing too little demand. There is demand out there, but it has not been growing at the same speed" as supply, Carole Nakhle, CEO of Crystol Energy says in a Welligence Energy Analytics webcast. "But prices have not crashed, which means that we are still able to absorb the additional barrels from OPEC+ and elsewhere." Economic data don't translate into strong demand growth, but China is still buying oil for storage. "It raises the question of how much further they can go accumulating all those barrels and for how long," Nakhle added. WTI is down 0.9% at $63.46 a barrel and Brent is 0.9% lower at $67.35. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0928 ET - Oil futures are flitting between small gains and losses as the market waits to see what additional sanctions the EU will impose against Russia. The possibility of disruptions in Russian oil supply has supported prices the past week, while concerns about higher OPEC+ output keeps a lid on gains. This week's large U.S. crude inventory draw and the Fed's interest rate cut "have helped to keep the losses in check," ING strategists Ewa Manthey and Warren Patterson say in a note. WTI is flat at $64.05 a barrel and Brent edges up 0.1% to $68.01. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1851 ET [Dow Jones]--Santos gets a new bear in Jarden after a consortium led by Abu Dhabi National Oil Co. unit XRG abandoned an $18.7 billion takeover offer. "We anticipate Santos share price weakness following the withdrawal of the US$5.626/share indicative offer (A$8.42/share)," analyst Nik Burns says. Santos ended Wednesday at A$7.65. That compares to Jarden's new A$7.05/share valuation of the stock, which removes any bid premium. Still, Jarden says it would expect investors to take advantage of any material selloff if they believe Santos's stock looks oversold. Jarden downgrades Santos to underweight, from overweight. (david.winning@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1444 ET - Gold futures fall further, with investors having the benefit of Fed Chair Powell's press conference following the central bank's 25 basis point rate cut Wednesday. "I don't think metals liked Powell's comment regarding his hardline stance against the idea of a 50 [basis-point] cut," says John Caruso of RJO Futures in a note. Caruso adds that longer-term, a Fed program with several rate cuts ahead is supportive for gold prices. Front-month gold futures finish down 1% to $3,643.70 a troy ounce, marking the second consecutive day that front-month gold settled lower. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1112 ET - BASF's 10-billion-euro investment in a new China plant has put considerable strain on its balance sheet, compromising the German chemical giant's dividend, BofA Securities analyst Matthew Yates says in a note. "We question the contribution BASF's new China plant will make to profits as it ramps up amidst depressed market conditions," he says. According to Yates, the plant also risks cannibalizing the European footprint due to shifting trade flows. This could lead to the need for more restructuring, "which BASF appears reluctant to contemplate, or can ill afford to do." BofA downgrades BASF to underperform from neutral. Shares trade 1.2% lower at 42.97 euros. (sarah.sloat@wsj.com)

0915 ET - Gold futures are backing away from a recent streak of record-high closes, with the most-active contract down 0.6%. But in the aftermath of the Fed's 25 basis point rate cut, many in the gold market think factors remain in place to push gold to new heights. "When rates are lower, we see yields drop and cash is less attractive, fueling gold demand," says Joseph Cavatoni of the World Gold Council. Cavatoni says that the WGC expects more demand for gold from western investors, although in the short-term volatility is expected--with some traders opting to lock in profits. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0352 ET - Malaysia's oil and gas sector upstream service providers may be entering an earnings down-cycle that could extend into 2026, Kenanga Investment Bank analyst Lim Sin Kiat says in a note. Although upstream service providers' share prices have fallen about 50% from the peak, he advises waiting for the down-cycle to run further before adopting a "buy the dip" strategy. Downstream earnings are expected to bottom in 2025, with global petrochemical supply adjustments potentially supporting a recovery in 2026, he adds. Kenanga recommends a barbell strategy anchored by defensive midstream stocks. He pegs Petronas Chemicals as a top pick and maintains a neutral rating on Malaysia's oil and gas sector. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0114 ET - Iluka Resources, now in "cash harvest mode," could consider offloading its stake in Deterra Royalties, according to Macquarie analysts. "While we understand divestment of DRR stake could result in capital gains tax, the mineral sands market headwind present another opportunity for ILU to review its portfolio and recycle capital," the analysts say in a note. Iluka has a 20% stake in Deterra, a business it spun off in 2020. In an August investor presentation, Iluka said Deterra provides it with an additional source of long-term financial strength. It estimated the value of its stake at A$445 million. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0053 ET - BHP's FY 2026 production guidance range for the BMA coal business is wide, which may give it some flexibility as it shutters the Saraji South mine, Morgan Stanley analysts say in a note. The miner estimates BMA FY 2026 output at 36 million-40 million metric tons, and says guidance is unchanged despite the decision to suspend low-margin Saraji South. The MS analysts say there is potential for additional tons to be produced elsewhere. Saraji South, part of the Saraji mine, has long haul distances, pushing up costs, they say. "The announced headcount reduction is likely to offset some negative impact to medium-term production." MS has an overweight rating and A$46.50 target on BHP. The stock is down 1.0% at A$39.905. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2139 ET - Northern Minerals' stock is too hot for Ord Minnett, which downgrades its recommendation to hold from speculative buy. Northern Minerals' valuation is up 90% in 2025 so far. But a definitive feasibility study into the Browns Range rare earths project in Australia wasn't as strong as Ord Minnett had hoped. In its base case, Northern Minerals forecasts a net present value of A$74 million after tax. That looks "soft relative to its market cap of A$326 million," analyst Matthew Hope says. The study suggests a payback of seven years from first production, with mining forecast to last 11 years. Ord Minnett says additional resources are needed. "These look likely given the Wolverine deposit remains open at depth, there are known satellite deposits, and Northern Minerals' tenement package is vast and largely unexplored," it says. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1935 ET - Dyno Nobel is expected by Jefferies to shutter its Phosphate Hill fertilizer plant following a review due to conclude by end-September. "Our base case is closure ahead of a major turnaround given [long-term] uncertainties around gas cost and sulphuric acid supply," Jefferies analysts say. Yet a big improvement in fertilizer prices complicates the decision. Diammonium phosphate prices are up 30% year to date. That signals much higher earnings than the market expected and implies significant dilution if Phosphate Hill is closed, say the analysts. "We estimate operating PH could add A$0.35/share through the next turnaround providing a major conundrum for management and shareholders." Jefferies has a hold rating on the stock and raises its target to A$3.15 from A$2.60. Dyno Nobel last traded at A$2.92. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

data.title: Banyan Announces Closing of Accelerated 100% Earn-In at the AurMac Project, Yukon, Canada
data.description:
VANCOUVER, BC / ACCESS Newswire / September 18, 2025 / Banyan Gold Corp. (the "Company" or "Banyan") (TSX-V:BYN)(OTCQB:BYAGF) is pleased to announce it has closed the 100% earn-in agreement on the AurMac Project ("AurMac") (the "Transaction"), previously announced on July 24th, 2025 with PricewaterhouseCoopers Inc., the receiver and manager (in such capacity, the "Receiver") for Victoria Gold Corp. ("Victoria"). Banyan had originally signed the option agreements on the properties in 2017, and by 2024, had earned 75%. The option agreements were originally to be completed in late 2025 and 2026. Pursuant to the previously announced definitive agreement to accelerate the options, Banyan has now earned the remaining interests (additional 25%) in the McQuesten and Aurex properties (which together comprise the core of the AurMac Project).

"With this closing, the strict timeline to complete the PEA is removed. Banyan will now be able to incorporate the results of our strategically targeted 2025 drilling into a PEA for 2026," stated Tara Christie, President and CEO." Banyan will now also upsize our 2025 drill program to over 40,000 metres."

AurMac hosts a gold Resource of 2.274 million ‎ounces of gold ("Au") (112.5 M tonnes at 0.63 g/t) and an Inferred Mineral Resource of 5.453 M oz of Au (280.6 M tonnes at 0.60 g/t )(see Table 1 below).

Upon closing of the Transaction, Banyan now owns 100% of the McQuesten and Aurex properties that contain the gold Resource.

Qualified Persons

Duncan Mackay, M.Sc., P.Geo., is a "Qualified Person" as ‎defined under National Instrument 43-101, Standards of Disclosure for Mineral Projects ("NI 43-101"), and has reviewed and approved the content of this news release in respect of all disclosure other than the MRE.‎ Mr. Mackay is Vice President Exploration for Banyan and has verified the data disclosed in this news release, including the sampling, ‎‎analytical and test data underlying the information.

Upcoming Events

Banyan's primary asset, the AurMac Project is located in the Traditional Territory of First Nation of Na-Cho Nyäk Dun, in Canada's Yukon Territory. data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1235 ET - More vessels loaded with grain are leaving the U.S. Gulf, according to data from the USDA. In its latest Grain Transportation Report, the agency says that an average 29 vessels were loaded with grain at a U.S. Gulf port per week over the past four weeks, versus an average of 24 vessels per week in the prior four weeks. Higher demand for U.S. corn exports is one explanation, the USDA says, reflecting what the agency says is "U.S. export competitiveness and robust early-season demand." Low prices per bushel and a weaker U.S. dollar are helping propel demand. Most-active CBOT corn futures are down 0.6%, while soybeans sink 0.7% and wheat falls 0.6%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1026 ET - Spirit Airlines is leaning on its pilots to find a way to save $100 million a year, according to a message from the pilots' union to members. The union told pilots that Spirit's financial situation has reached a "critical point" and change is coming, whether through a negotiated agreement, a judge's order, or Spirit's liquidation. The company has told the pilots that if there's no agreement by Oct. 1, it intends to seek relief from the court, where the pilots' contract could be rejected. (alison.sider@wsj.com; @alyrose)

1022 ET - Spirit Airlines is contracting. The airline, which last month filed for bankruptcy less than a year after emerging from a previous bankruptcy, plans to shrink its schedule by 25% from a year ago to focus on its strongest markets, CEO Dave Davis tells employees in a memo. Spirit's first chapter 11 failed to address all of the airlines' financial issues, including costly airplane leases. Now the carrier is evaluating how many planes it will need for its smaller footprint and plans to meet with unions to discuss cost-cutting measures, Davis says. "These evaluations will inevitably affect the size of our teams as we become a more efficient airline." (alison.sider@wsj.com; @alyrose)

0655 ET - Grab is expected to strengthen its market leadership, HSBC analysts say in a note. The bank forecasts the ride-hailing and delivery platform's on-demand gross merchandise value to grow 19% in 2025 and at a compound annual growth rate of 17.2% over 2024-2027. "We expect margins to improve with increased contributions from high-value offerings, scale and advertising revenue," HSBC says. However, HSBC downgrades Grab to hold from buy following the stock's 77% gain over the past 12 months and on its view that the risk-reward is balanced. The bank raises its target price to $6.20 from $6.00 on likely market-share gains in the on-demand segment. Grab last closed at $6.10. (jason.chau@wsj.com)

0438 ET - Bollore's new investment opportunities after the Vivendi split and solid first-half profitability ease some concerns over its legal risks, Baader analysts write. The French conglomerate posted mixed first-half earnings Tuesday following the deconsolidation of its Vivendi holding, but saw strong profitability thanks to the inclusion of resulting spinoffs. Bollore also reduced its Universal Music Group stake, which may be excessive, the analysts say. It could deploy those shares in a Vivendi takeover, Baader says. Still, litigation risks remain following fraud complaints over port concessions made in March by groups from five African countries. Shares are down 0.7% at 4.8 euros. (william.gray@wsj.com)

0407 ET - Geely Automobile's new Galaxy M9 sports-utility vehicle is likely to book higher sales volumes than its close peer by Chongqing Changan Automobile, says Deutsche Bank analyst Bin Wang in a note. The SUV launched with an "impressively competitive price," much lower than Changan's newest SUV, he says. He estimates Geely's new offering to sell an average of 5,000 units a month, above the Changan SUV's monthly average of around 2,900 units in the past three months. He therefore raises his 2025 net profit estimate for Geely by 0.4%. The bank raises its target on Geely to HK$26.00 from HK$25.00 and maintains a buy rating. Shares fall 4.8% to HK$18.81. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0144 ET - Airports of Thailand's shares could gain if the airport manager raises its passenger service charges for domestic and international travelers, says DBS Group Research's Nantika Wiangphoem in a note. The company is submitting its study on raising its passenger service charges to the Thai authorities, with the new rates likely to be effective in February if approved. The analyst expects the higher charge could be more positive than the market expects, with room to rise further. She lifts her FY 2026 earnings forecast by 15%, factoring in an estimated 100 baht hike for domestic and international passengers. DBS raises its rating on AOT to hold from fully valued, and increases the target price to THB38.00 from THB32.00. Shares last at THB39.75. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0112 ET - Samsung Heavy Industries' robust earnings are likely to continue into 2028, says CGS International's Brian Park in a note. The South Korean shipbuilder's 2028 revenue and operating profit should gain from a better margin mix and stable ship-construction prices, they say. The company likely remains a key beneficiary of liquefied natural gas carrier orders from the U.S. after the Trump administration resumed export permits. This could support orders for 2029 delivery and beyond. Park also raises his 2025-2027 earnings per share forecast by 2.0%-6.6% to reflect likely faster production progress in the offshore segment. CGS International raises the stock's target to KRW30,000 from KRW26,000 and reiterates an add rating. Shares are down 1.4% at KRW21,250. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0051 ET - Yangzijiang Shipbuilding's business outlook appears upbeat, Daiwa Capital Markets analysts say, citing a recent call with the company's management. The shipbuilder is riding on strong demand for containership feeders, the analysts note. Its small-sized containership orders have outperformed, rising more than 60% on year for the first 8 months of 2025, the analysts say. Yangzijiang Shipbuilding looks well-placed to capture robust feeder demand thanks to the wavier of upcoming U.S. port fees on China ships and earlier available delivery slots. Daiwa lifts its 2025-2027 EPS forecasts for the company by 7%-41%. It raises the stock's target price to S$3.90 from S$3.35, with an unchanged buy rating. Shares are 1.6% higher at S$3.20. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2350 ET - Thai Airways International's stock is likely to warrant a premium valuation, UOB Kay Hian analysts say in a research report. The analysts cite the airline's fleet additions of fuel-efficient narrow-body aircraft, falling maintenance costs owing to fewer engine types, and declining fuel costs due to the downtrend in oil prices. Other supportive factors include the airline's increasing number of high-margin seats via cabin retrofits and lower employee costs stemming from corporate restructuring. The brokerage initiates coverage of the stock with a buy rating and a target price of THB17.00. Shares are 1.4% lower at THB14.30. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2250 ET - HSBC analysts forecast 20% lower earnings for BYD from 2025-2027 on lower volume growth but see better longer-term prospects. The carmaker's absorption of higher autonomous driving costs and price discipline versus more aggressive rivals amid a government crackdown on unhealthy competition are weighing on earnings. "We expect a rope-a-dope approach in 2H," the analysts add, projecting lower 2025 sales volume and 2H sales growth. HSBC expects a turnaround in 2026, with volume growth boosted by a potential platform tech upgrade and vertical integration. A projected 50% overseas sales growth next year should also support earnings, the bank notes. HSBC maintains buy ratings on BYD's H and A shares, but lowers H share target to HK$144.00 from HK$151.00 and its A share target to CNY131.00 from CNY143.00.(jason.chau@wsj.com)

2049 ET - Singapore Airlines is likely to report a major fall in 2Q earnings for the fiscal year ending March 2026, UOB Kay Hian's Roy Chen says in a note. While the airline's operating profit is likely to grow 10% on the year, that will likely be more than offset by the drag from subsidiary Air India and declining interest income, he says. He estimates 2Q net profit at S$100 million-S$200 million, compared to S$290 million in 2Q of fiscal 2025. He reckons earnings will stay in decline for the rest of the financial year, weighing on SIA's shares. UOB KH raises its target on the stock to S$6.05 from S$6.03. The brokerage maintains a sell rating on SIA, which last traded at S$6.52. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

data.title: Margin Debt Has Soared. It's the Skunk at Wall Street's Garden Party. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Jacob Sonenshine

The dollars that investors borrow to buy stocks has jumped, a risk to the market.

With what's commonly called margin debt, an investor buys $100 worth of stock by borrowing $50 from the broker, for example.

Only $50 is the investor's initial equity investment. If the stock price climbs to $120, the investor's return is 40% because the $20 of profit came on $50 of investment. So margin debt amplifies returns.

It works the same way with losses. If that $100 worth of stock drops to $80, the loss is 40% on the $50 of investment.

Neither the government nor brokerages allow losses to become too large. The rule of the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (Finra) is that equity in margin accounts can't drop below 25% of the value of the holdings.

If a stock price falls enough that the margin account falls below 25%, the investor must deposit more cash into the account to abide by FINRA's threshold. To do that, the investor usually has to sell shares to raise enough cash.

That's why market declines can become steeper as margin debt rises. Investors rush to sell the minute the value of their stocks falls too close to Finra's 25% threshold.

Margin debt, according to Finra, stands at $1.1 trillion — up 69% from the 2022 low of roughly $650 billion and nearing the record high of almost $1.2 trillion set in 2021. Every time the value of the market jumps, investors can borrow more because each borrowed dollar represents a lower percentage of the value of stocks. Consequently, their margin accounts aren't out of control and their debt balances balloon.

That's why it's important to note that debt dollars, despite their increase, aren't so high compared to the size of the market. Margin debt is still only 1.9% of an S&P 500 worth $56 trillion, according to FactSet. That's down from a high for this century of almost 3.5%, according to research firm First Trust Economics.

The concern is that risk is slowly building. Margin debt as a percentage of the S&P 500 hit a multidecade low of 1.6% last year and has begun to creep higher to its current level.

"This latest jump warrants caution," write analysts at First Trust Economics.

To that point, it takes a mere single-digit percentage drop in the S&P 500 to bring margin debt to above 2% of the index. After any brief dip, some traders will see the equity values of their accounts drop too low, forcing more selling — and more market declines.

Consistent with that, surging debt dollars tend to cause more-than-minor market declines. Margin debt balances hit record highs in 2000, 2007, and 2021, according to ICAP Technical Analysis. The S&P 500 drops that came months after those points were all greater than 20%.

That type of drop doesn't have to happen today. But the point is that the market is vulnerable to some sort of fall, with a faint possibility of a worse-than-20% drawdown. Margin debt is close to a record and becoming a large portion of traders' accounts.

A selloff won't begin until an economic catalyst emerges. On Thursday, the three major indexes were in the green, dancing with abandon to the Federal Reserve's first rate cut this year and the hope that the central bank will cut more to keep the economy growing.

That won't happen, though, if inflation remains above the Fed's 2% annual target for long enough. Fewer — or no — additional cuts would pressure the market.

Another scenario would be economic data that fails to meet expectations before lower rates boost the economy. That could also hurt the market.

If a catalyst emerges, the selling could become exaggerated because of the extraordinary amount of margin debt.

It's a sticky wicket, to be sure. But this is for sure: Risk to the market is becoming more pronounced, and buying more stock today just isn't the best idea.

Write to Jacob Sonenshine at jacob.sonenshine@barrons.com

NGL Energy Partners LP announced today that the Board of Directors of its general partner declared a distribution for the quarter ending September 30, 2025 to be paid to the holders of the Partnership's 11.765% Class B Floating Rate Cumulative Redeemable Perpetual Preferred Units ("Class B Preferred Units") and the 11.675% Class C Floating Rate Cumulative Redeemable Perpetual Preferred Units ("Class C Preferred Units") in accordance with the terms outlined in NGL's partnership agreement. Each of the Class B Preferred Units will receive a quarterly distribution of $0.7353 and each of the Class C Preferred Units will receive a quarterly distribution of $0.7297 per unit on October 15, 2025, to holders of record on October 1, 2025.

Additionally, the Board of Directors declared a quarterly cash distribution for the Class D Preferred Units in the amount of $15,568,183.02 for the quarter ending September 30, 2025. The Class D Preferred distribution will also be made on October 15, 2025, to the holders of record on October 1, 2025.

Chimera is a publicly traded real estate investment trust, or REIT, whose principal business objective is to provide attractive risk-adjusted returns and distributable income through investment performance linked to mortgage credit fundamentals. data.title: CFSB Bancorp, Inc. /MA/ SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
CFSB Bancorp, Inc., a federal corporation formed in January 2022 to serve as the savings and loan holding company for Colonial Federal Savings Bank, has released its annual 10-K report. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges and risks it faces.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Interest and Dividend Income: $13.1 million, reflecting a 12.2% increase from the previous year due to higher interest and dividends on taxable debt securities and short-term investments.
  • Net Interest Income: $7.0 million, an increase of 4.1% from the prior year, driven by an increase in the average balance of interest-earning assets and a higher average rate earned on these assets.
  • Net (Loss) Income: $(265,000), compared to a net income of $33,000 in the previous year, primarily due to an increase in the provision for credit losses and higher non-interest expenses.
  • Net (Loss) Income Per Share - Basic: $(0.04), compared to $0.01 in the previous year, reflecting the net loss incurred during the year.
  • Net (Loss) Income Per Share - Diluted: $(0.04), consistent with the basic earnings per share, as there were no dilutive effects.

Business Highlights

  • Company Overview: CFSB Bancorp, Inc. primarily engages in holding the common stock of Colonial Federal Savings Bank and supporting its operations.
  • Market Area: The company operates primarily in Norfolk and Plymouth Counties, Massachusetts, focusing on residential suburbs of Boston. Norfolk County is noted for its high median household income and educational attainment.
  • Lending Activities: The primary lending activity is one- to four-family residential real estate loans, which constitute 79.4% of the total loan portfolio. The company also engages in multi-family and commercial real estate lending, with a focus on diversifying its loan portfolio.
  • Loan Portfolio Composition: As of June 30, 2025, the loan portfolio includes $142.3 million in one- to four-family residential loans, $16.4 million in multi-family loans, and $14.3 million in commercial real estate loans.
  • Geographical Performance: The company’s operations are concentrated in Norfolk County, Massachusetts, characterized by a high concentration of white-collar professionals and a low unemployment rate compared to the national average.
  • Competition: The company faces significant competition from various financial institutions, including large banks, community banks, and credit unions, particularly in the areas of loan origination and deposit attraction.
  • Regulatory Environment: CFSB Bancorp and its bank are subject to comprehensive regulation by federal agencies, including the OCC and the Federal Reserve Board, impacting their operational activities and strategic decisions.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to continue focusing on growing its balance sheet and improving profitability by emphasizing the origination of multi-family and commercial real estate loans, subject to market conditions.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Merger Agreement: The company entered into a Merger Agreement with Hometown Financial Group, MHC, which includes a series of mergers culminating in the Bank merging with North Shore Bank. This strategic initiative aims to enhance the company's market position and operational scale.
  • Capital Management: The company repurchased $495,000 of its own stock during the year ended June 30, 2025. Additionally, the company maintained a strong liquidity position with a net increase in deposits of $3.3 million and had $10.4 million in Federal Home Loan Bank advances. The company also had the ability to borrow an additional $53.0 million from the Federal Home Loan Bank of Boston.
  • Future Outlook: The company anticipates having sufficient funds to meet its current funding commitments, with plans to increase loans supported by an increase in core deposits and supplemented by Federal Home Loan Bank advances as needed. The merger with Hometown Financial Group is expected to provide further strategic growth opportunities.

Challenges and Risks

  • Merger-Related Risks: The pending merger with Hometown Financial Group presents uncertainties that could impair CFSB Bancorp's ability to attract and retain key personnel and maintain customer relationships. The merger agreement restricts certain business actions, potentially limiting business opportunities. Significant costs related to the merger are expected, and there is no assurance that the anticipated benefits will offset these costs.
  • Regulatory Risks: The merger requires approvals from various regulatory authorities, which may impose conditions that could delay completion or increase costs. Failure to complete the merger by March 31, 2026, allows either party to terminate the agreement, potentially impacting CFSB Bancorp's future business and financial results.
  • Lending Risks: A downturn in the local real estate market could negatively impact profitability, as almost all loans are secured by real estate. The company also faces risks related to its allowance for credit losses, which may not be sufficient to cover actual loan losses, especially as it increases its portfolio of multi-family and commercial real estate loans.
  • Economic Risks: Inflation and adverse economic conditions in the primary market area could adversely affect financial performance. The company is particularly vulnerable to local economic downturns due to its high concentration of real estate-secured loans in Norfolk and Plymouth Counties, Massachusetts.
  • Operational Risks: The company relies heavily on technology, exposing it to risks of system failures, security breaches, and cyber-attacks. Additionally, reliance on third-party vendors introduces further cybersecurity risks.
  • Regulatory Compliance Risks: Non-compliance with laws such as the USA PATRIOT Act and Bank Secrecy Act could result in fines or sanctions. Changes in laws and regulations could also increase operational costs.
  • Market Risks: The company faces intense competition in its market area, which may limit growth and profitability. Its smaller asset size compared to competitors may hinder its ability to compete effectively.
